metrics.name: Wale
metrics.question: Will Matthew Wale be elected as the next Prime Minister of Solomon Islands following the May 2026 no-confidence vote?

reversal_search.why: every actor already holds a reversal-capable state

simulation_result.question: Will Matthew Wale be elected as the next Prime Minister of Solomon Islands following the May 2026 no-confidence vote?
limitations: SIMULATION READINESS GATE STOP (not_ready): actor_weights:john_agovaka: weight sum 0.9999; actor_weights:opposition_coalition_mps: weight sum 0.9999; terminal_round_trip: synthetic YES→1 / NO→0 through the live path

rollout was refused BEFORE any actor call — the world cannot yet be proven to reach its measured outcome; the failing checks above are the exact repairs needed (this is a loud structural failure, never a silent prior-only forecast)
unrepaired validator failures: action vote_for_pm requires authority ['mp status'] that none of its actors hold; trigger names unknown actor 'parliament'
no resolved rollout mass: the headline is the grounded_reference_prior (0.167) — simulated-world confirmation is absent; treat as exploratory

under_modeled_components.why: synthetic YES→1 / NO→0 through the live path

limitations: no resolved rollout mass: the headline is the grounded_reference_prior (0.167) — simulated-world confirmation is absent; treat as exploratory

blueprint.causal_thesis: Matthew Wale, as opposition leader, seeks to convert his coalition's parliamentary majority into his own election as Prime Minister, but fluid coalition politics and rival candidates like John Agovaka may produce a compromise candidate, determining the outcome.

validation_failures_final.what: action vote_for_pm requires authority ['mp status'] that none of its actors hold

terminal_canonicalization.why: vote terminals read the tally directly

pruned.reason: ceremonial: no discretion over any surviving terminal pathway

solomon_islands_coalition_instability.claim: Solomon Islands coalition politics are notoriously fluid; the leader of a no-confidence motion does not always become PM, as coalition negotiations can produce compromise candidates.

provenance.definition: Solomon Islands coalition politics are notoriously fluid; the leader of a no-confidence motion does not always become PM, as coalition negotiations can produce compromise candidates.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 2 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: In 2019, after a no-confidence motion against Prime Minister Rick Hou, the opposition coalition elected Manasseh Sogavare as PM, not the motion's leader.
cases.basis_quote: Manasseh Sogavare was elected prime minister by parliament on 24 April 2019, replacing Rick Hou, after a no-confidence vote.

cases.description: In 2014, after the general election, a coalition led by the Democratic Alliance Party elected Manasseh Sogavare as PM, bypassing other candidates.
cases.basis_quote: Manasseh Sogavare was elected prime minister by parliament on 9 December 2014, heading a coalition government.

china_taiwan_diplomatic_rivalry.claim: Solomon Islands' shift to China in 2019 and subsequent tensions with Taiwan create external pressure on leadership choices, affecting coalition stability.

provenance.definition: Solomon Islands' shift to China in 2019 and subsequent tensions with Taiwan create external pressure on leadership choices, affecting coalition stability.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: In 2019, Prime Minister Manasseh Sogavare switched diplomatic recognition from Taiwan to China, leading to internal political turmoil and a no-confidence vote.
cases.basis_quote: Solomon Islands switches diplomatic recognition from Taiwan to China, prompting political crisis.

economic_dependence_on_aid.claim: Solomon Islands relies heavily on foreign aid, particularly from China and Australia, which influences political alignments and coalition formation.

provenance.definition: Solomon Islands relies heavily on foreign aid, particularly from China and Australia, which influences political alignments and coalition formation.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: In 2021, the Solomon Islands government accepted a security pact with China, reflecting aid-driven alignment.
cases.basis_quote: Solomon Islands signs security pact with China, deepening ties amid aid dependence.

matthew_wale.quantity: Matthew Wale is elected Prime Minister

provenance.definition: Matthew Wale is formally elected or appointed as Prime Minister of Solomon Islands and sworn in by the Governor-General.
provenance.inclusion_criteria: Official announcement by Solomon Islands Government or credible news reporting (ABC News Australia, RNZ, etc.) that Matthew Wale has been elected and sworn in as Prime Minister.
provenance.exclusion_criteria: Any other individual elected; no election by August 1, 2026; Wale not sworn in.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: Matthew Wale was elected as Leader of the Opposition in 2019, but never became PM in prior no-confidence attempts.
cases.basis_quote: Matthew Wale elected as Leader of the Opposition, but not Prime Minister.

frederick_kologeto.quantity: Frederick Kologeto is elected Prime Minister

provenance.definition: Frederick Kologeto is formally elected or appointed as Prime Minister and sworn in.
provenance.inclusion_criteria: Official announcement or credible reporting that Frederick Kologeto has been elected and sworn in as Prime Minister.
provenance.exclusion_criteria: Any other individual elected; no election by deadline.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: Frederick Kologeto was a candidate for Prime Minister in 2019 but lost to Manasseh Sogavare.
cases.basis_quote: Frederick Kologeto was a candidate for prime minister but was not elected.

jeremiah_manele.quantity: Jeremiah Manele is re-elected Prime Minister

provenance.definition: Jeremiah Manele is re-elected as Prime Minister after the no-confidence vote.
provenance.inclusion_criteria: Official announcement or credible reporting that Jeremiah Manele has been re-elected and sworn in as Prime Minister.
provenance.exclusion_criteria: Any other individual elected; no election by deadline.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: Jeremiah Manele was elected Prime Minister in 2024.
cases.basis_quote: Jeremiah Manele elected prime minister in 2024.

cases.description: Jeremiah Manele ousted via no-confidence on May 7, 2026.
cases.basis_quote: Jeremiah Manele ousted via no-confidence vote.

outcome_reference_class.quantity: Matthew Wale is elected Prime Minister

provenance.definition: Matthew Wale is formally elected or appointed as Prime Minister of Solomon Islands and sworn in by the Governor-General on or after May 7, 2026.
provenance.inclusion_criteria: Official announcement by Solomon Islands Government or credible news reporting (ABC News Australia, RNZ, etc.) that Matthew Wale has been elected and sworn in as Prime Minister.
provenance.exclusion_criteria: Any other individual elected; no election by August 1, 2026; Wale not sworn in.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 2 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: In 2019, after a no-confidence vote, Manasseh Sogavare was elected PM, not the opposition leader.
cases.basis_quote: Manasseh Sogavare elected prime minister after no-confidence vote, not opposition leader.

cases.description: In 2014, after general election, Manasseh Sogavare elected PM from coalition.
cases.basis_quote: Manasseh Sogavare elected prime minister from coalition.

parliament_of_solomon_islands.consequence_of_nonparticipation: If no new PM elected by August 1, 2026, the question resolves No.

weight_invariant.rule: every weight traces to a counted reference class or is explicit unknown mass; no qualitative label is mapped to a number

reversal_search.why: every actor already holds a reversal-capable state

john_agovaka.residual_provenance: uncovered feasible option(s) ['abstain', 'frederick kologeto', 'jeremiah manele'] with no counted cases — bounded per option

opposition_coalition_mps.residual_provenance: uncovered feasible option(s) ['abstain', 'jeremiah manele', 'john agovaka'] with no counted cases — bounded per option

matthew_wale.claim: Matthew Wale believes he has secured enough cross-party commitments to become Prime Minister, but he privately doubts the durability of these promises due to past betrayals in Solomon Islands politics.
beliefs: Coalition partners are unreliable and may defect under external pressure from China or Taiwan.
His legal victory forcing the no-confidence vote gives him moral authority but not guaranteed votes.
goals: Secure the premiership to advance anti-corruption reforms.
Avoid making concessions that alienate his core supporters.
matthew_wale.pressures: Intense lobbying from foreign embassies and local faction leaders demanding patronage.
stances: Publicly projects unity; privately distrusts Frederick Kologeto's coalition.
Seeks to balance diplomatic relations with both China and Taiwan to maximize aid.
historical_case_refs: 2019 Solomon Islands switch from Taiwan to China causing internal splits
Multiple no-confidence votes in PNG and SI leading to short-lived PMs
distinguishing_observations: Wale's private meetings with MPs from both sides of the aisle have been unusually secretive.
He has not publicly named any coalition partners, unlike Kologeto.
matthew_wale.action_if_state: Wale will aggressively court undecided MPs with policy promises and minor cabinet posts, but will avoid making any firm commitments on foreign policy until after the vote.

matthew_wale.claim: Matthew Wale privately believes he lacks the numbers to win and is already positioning himself as the opposition leader for the next government, focusing on preserving his political future.
beliefs: The 26-member winning coalition is solidly behind Kologeto or another candidate.
His own party is fractured and some members have been bribed by foreign interests.
goals: Avoid a humiliating defeat that could end his career.
Ensure he remains a credible opposition figure to contest the next election.
matthew_wale.pressures: Party elders urging him to step aside for a younger candidate if he loses.
stances: Publicly maintains he is the frontrunner; privately instructs allies to prepare for a loss.
Will not challenge the outcome if defeated, to preserve institutional norms.
historical_case_refs: 2014 SI election where opposition leader failed to form government despite early momentum
2021 ouster of Prime Minister Sogavare's predecessor after short tenure
distinguishing_observations: Wale has not scheduled any major campaign rallies or media appearances.
His close aides have been seen meeting with foreign diplomats without him.
matthew_wale.action_if_state: Wale will quietly negotiate with Kologeto's camp for a senior role in the next government, such as Deputy Prime Minister, in exchange for not contesting the vote.

matthew_wale.claim: Matthew Wale believes he can win if the vote is delayed, and is using procedural tactics to buy time to flip MPs, while privately considering a deal with China to secure their support.
beliefs: A delay of even a few days could allow him to peel off MPs from Kologeto's coalition.
China's influence in Solomon Islands is decisive and can be leveraged for personal gain.
goals: Become Prime Minister at any cost, even if it means making secret commitments to Beijing.
Undermine Kologeto's coalition by exposing internal divisions.
matthew_wale.pressures: Australian and US diplomats warning against deepening ties with China.
stances: Publicly calls for a fair and transparent process; privately instructs allies to raise procedural objections.
Signals openness to Chinese infrastructure loans in exchange for political support.
historical_case_refs: 2000 SI coup where political delays led to foreign intervention
2022 China-SI security pact negotiations that were kept secret until last minute
distinguishing_observations: Wale's legal team has filed motions questioning the eligibility of several MPs in the opposing coalition.
He has made uncharacteristically warm statements about Chinese investment in recent days.
matthew_wale.action_if_state: Wale will use parliamentary procedure to postpone the prime ministerial vote, while secretly dispatching an envoy to Beijing to negotiate a support package.

john_agovaka.claim: John Agovaka privately believes Matthew Wale is too weak to hold the coalition together and will quickly lose the premiership, so Agovaka positions himself as the steady alternative who can actually govern.
beliefs: Matthew Wale lacks the political muscle to manage the fractious coalition and will be ousted within months.
A short Wale premiership will discredit the opposition and open a path for Agovaka as a unifying figure.
goals: Avoid openly backing Wale to preserve his own reputation as a viable PM candidate.
Quietly signal to coalition MPs that he is ready to step in if Wale falters.
john_agovaka.pressures: Institutional incentives to secure a majority push him to appear cooperative, but his personal ambition and assessment of Wale's fragility make him reluctant to fully commit.
stances: Publicly neutral or lukewarm toward Wale's candidacy.
Privately encourages coalition partners to keep Wale on a short leash.
supporting_evidence_ids: John Agovaka is identified as a likely PM candidate, indicating he has personal ambitions beyond the current race.
Solomon Islands coalition instability is a known condition, making leadership fragility plausible.
historical_case_refs: Solomon Islands has seen rapid turnover of PMs in coalition governments, e.g., Sogavare's multiple ousters.
distinguishing_observations: Agovaka does not actively campaign for Wale despite being a senior opposition figure.
He makes ambiguous statements about 'unity' and 'experience' that could be read as criticism of Wale.
john_agovaka.action_if_state: Agovaka withholds full public endorsement of Wale, instead positioning himself as a potential consensus candidate if the coalition deadlocks, and privately lobbies MPs to consider him as a backup.

john_agovaka.claim: John Agovaka privately believes Matthew Wale is the best chance to secure Chinese aid and stabilize the economy, so he fully backs Wale and works to ensure his victory.
beliefs: Wale's long experience as opposition leader makes him the most credible PM to negotiate with China and maintain aid flows.
A stable Wale government is essential to prevent economic collapse and further diplomatic chaos.
goals: Ensure Wale is elected with a clear majority to avoid prolonged uncertainty.
Secure a senior cabinet position (e.g., Finance or Foreign Affairs) to influence aid policy.
john_agovaka.pressures: Economic dependence on aid and the China-Taiwan rivalry create strong incentives for a leader who can maintain ties with Beijing; Agovaka sees Wale as that leader.
stances: Publicly and privately advocates for Wale as the only viable candidate.
Actively mediates between coalition factions to lock in votes for Wale.
supporting_evidence_ids: Economic dependence on aid is a shared world condition, making aid continuity a priority.
China-Taiwan diplomatic rivalry is in play, so a PM with experience in that arena is valued.
historical_case_refs: Solomon Islands switched recognition to China in 2019 under Sogavare, and aid has been a key factor in leadership choice
distinguishing_observations: Agovaka is seen coordinating with Wale's campaign team and publicly praising his leadership.
He uses his influence to bring undecided MPs into Wale's camp.
john_agovaka.action_if_state: Agovaka becomes Wale's chief whip, securing the 26-vote majority by brokering deals with smaller parties and ensuring discipline within the coalition.

john_agovaka.claim: John Agovaka privately believes the coalition is too unstable for any PM to last, so he focuses on building his own profile to become the eventual successor, regardless of who wins now.
beliefs: The current coalition will fracture within a year, leading to another no-confidence vote.
By staying above the fray, he can emerge as the clean, competent alternative after the inevitable collapse.
goals: Avoid being tied to a losing or short-lived government.
Cultivate relationships with foreign donors and domestic power brokers independently of Wale or Kologeto.
john_agovaka.pressures: Institutional incentives to gain a majority push him to join a faction, but his long-term calculation favors independence until the dust settles.
stances: Publicly non-committal, emphasizing 'due process' and 'stability' without endorsing any candidate.
Privately meets with diplomats and business leaders to present himself as a future PM.
supporting_evidence_ids: Solomon Islands coalition instability is a known condition, making early collapse plausible.
Agovaka is identified as a likely PM candidate, suggesting he is positioning for the future.
historical_case_refs: In 2021, Manasseh Sogavare survived multiple no-confidence votes before eventually being ousted; many MPs positioned the
distinguishing_observations: Agovaka avoids making any firm commitment to either Wale or Kologeto in public statements.
He is seen holding private meetings with foreign embassy officials without coalition leaders present.
john_agovaka.action_if_state: Agovaka votes for the candidate most likely to lose quickly (e.g., Wale if he expects a short tenure), then immediately begins building a cross-factional alliance for the next no-confidence vote, posi

frederick_kologeto.claim: Frederick Kologeto privately believes that Matthew Wale is the only candidate who can secure enough cross-party support to form a stable government, and that backing Wale is the best way to ensure his own party retains influence in the new cabinet.
beliefs: Matthew Wale's long tenure as Opposition Leader gives him the necessary experience and relationships to build a coalition.
Supporting Wale will allow the People's First Party to secure key ministerial posts and policy concessions.
A prolonged leadership contest would destabilize the country and harm the party's reputation.
goals: Secure a stable government that includes People's First Party in a meaningful role.
Avoid a snap election that could weaken his party's position.
Maintain unity within the winning coalition.
frederick_kologeto.pressures: Pressure from coalition partners to quickly elect a leader and from international donors who want political stability.
stances: Strongly supports Matthew Wale for Prime Minister.
Opposes any candidate who would exclude his party from power.
supporting_evidence_ids: The 26-member winning coalition must now elect a new Prime Minister, with a vote expected in the week of May 11, 2026
Matthew Wale, the long-term Leader of the Opposition, who was instrumental in the legal challenges that forced the no-confidence vote
historical_case_refs: Similar coalition-building in Solomon Islands after the 2019 no-confidence vote against Manasseh Sogavare, where a compr
distinguishing_observations: Kologeto has not publicly endorsed any candidate yet, but his private meetings with Wale have been reported.
His party's MPs are divided between Wale and another candidate.
frederick_kologeto.action_if_state: Kologeto will actively campaign for Wale within the coalition, using his influence to sway undecided MPs and negotiate a power-sharing agreement that guarantees his party's role.

frederick_kologeto.claim: Frederick Kologeto privately believes he himself should be the next Prime Minister, viewing the no-confidence motion as a stepping stone to his own elevation, and is working behind the scenes to position himself as the unity candidate.
beliefs: As the mover of the no-confidence motion, he has the political capital to claim the premiership.
His party is the largest in the coalition, giving him a strong claim to lead.
Matthew Wale is too closely associated with the previous opposition and would be divisive.
goals: Become Prime Minister of Solomon Islands.
Consolidate power within the People's First Party and the coalition.
Marginalize rivals like Wale.
frederick_kologeto.pressures: Pressure from within his party to claim the top job, but also from coalition partners who may see him as too ambitious or inexperienced.
stances: Opposes Matthew Wale's candidacy.
Promotes himself as a fresh alternative.
supporting_evidence_ids: The no-confidence motion was tabled by a coalition led by Frederick Kologeto of the People's First Party.
Two leading candidates have emerged for the premiership: Matthew Wale...
historical_case_refs: In 2019, Manasseh Sogavare used a no-confidence motion to return as Prime Minister after being ousted earlier.
distinguishing_observations: Kologeto has been unusually quiet in public, avoiding endorsements.
His allies have been floating his name as a potential compromise candidate.
frederick_kologeto.action_if_state: Kologeto will secretly lobby MPs to support his own candidacy, offering ministerial positions and other incentives, while publicly maintaining neutrality to avoid appearing power-hungry.

frederick_kologeto.claim: Frederick Kologeto privately believes that no stable government can be formed from the current parliament, and that triggering a snap election is the best way for his party to win an outright majority and govern without coalition constraints.
beliefs: The current coalition is too fractured to govern effectively.
His party's popularity has grown since the no-confidence vote, and an election would increase their seats.
Matthew Wale's candidacy would lead to a weak government that would collapse quickly.
goals: Force a snap election within months.
Win a majority for the People's First Party.
Avoid being part of a coalition that limits his party's agenda.
frederick_kologeto.pressures: Pressure from party hardliners who want an election, but also from international donors who fear instability.
stances: Opposes any candidate who can form a stable government.
Privately encourages deadlock in the prime ministerial vote.
supporting_evidence_ids: Solomon Islands coalition instability is a known factor.
The no-confidence vote passed 26-22, indicating a narrow and potentially fragile majority.
historical_case_refs: In 2007, a no-confidence vote in Solomon Islands led to a prolonged political crisis and eventually a snap election.
distinguishing_observations: Kologeto has been critical of the coalition's ability to govern in private conversations.
His party has been running campaign-style events despite the ongoing parliamentary process.
frederick_kologeto.action_if_state: Kologeto will instruct his MPs to vote against any candidate, including Wale, to prevent a quorum or majority, and will use procedural delays to run out the clock until an election is called.

jeremiah_manele.claim: Jeremiah Manele believes he can negotiate a secret agreement with Matthew Wale's coalition to return as Prime Minister after a brief interim, leveraging his remaining loyalists and foreign backers.
beliefs: His ouster is temporary and reversible through back-channel deals.
Matthew Wale's coalition is fragile and can be split with promises of ministerial posts.
goals: Regain the Prime Ministership within weeks.
Preserve his faction's influence in the coalition government.
jeremiah_manele.pressures: Intense lobbying from China to maintain a friendly government, and from domestic allies who fear losing patronage.
stances: Opposes Matthew Wale's permanent premiership.
Open to a power-sharing arrangement that keeps him in leadership.
contradicting_evidence_ids: The 26-member winning coalition must now elect a new Prime Minister
historical_case_refs: Solomon Islands 2021 no-confidence vote where Manasseh Sogavare returned after a brief ouster.
distinguishing_observations: Manele has not publicly conceded defeat and is reportedly meeting with MPs from the winning coalition.
jeremiah_manele.action_if_state: Manele will secretly negotiate with defectors in Wale's coalition, offering cabinet positions and aid promises, while publicly calling for a unity government.

jeremiah_manele.claim: Jeremiah Manele accepts his ouster as final and shifts focus to positioning himself for the next election cycle, cooperating with the new government to maintain relevance.
beliefs: The no-confidence vote reflects a genuine loss of majority support.
Fighting the outcome would damage his long-term political career.
goals: Ensure a smooth transition to preserve his party's institutional role.
Rebuild his political base for the 2027 general election.
jeremiah_manele.pressures: Public and international pressure to respect democratic processes; desire to avoid political isolation.
stances: Accepts Matthew Wale as Prime Minister.
Seeks to be a constructive opposition leader.
supporting_evidence_ids: Solomon Islands to get new leader after Jeremiah Manele voted out ...
historical_case_refs: Danny Philip's peaceful transition after losing a no-confidence vote in 2011.
distinguishing_observations: Manele has not made any public statements challenging the vote's legitimacy.
jeremiah_manele.action_if_state: Manele will issue a statement conceding defeat, pledge cooperation with Wale's government, and begin organizing his party for the next election.

jeremiah_manele.claim: Jeremiah Manele believes the no-confidence vote was procedurally flawed and will challenge its validity in court, aiming to nullify the result and remain Prime Minister.
beliefs: The vote was conducted in violation of parliamentary rules.
The courts will side with him based on precedent.
goals: Overturn the no-confidence vote.
Retain power through judicial intervention.
jeremiah_manele.pressures: Legal advice from allies; risk of political crisis if challenge fails.
stances: Rejects the legitimacy of the vote.
Seeks to delay the election of a new Prime Minister.
contradicting_evidence_ids: The 26-member winning coalition must now elect a new Prime Minister
historical_case_refs: 2000 Fiji coup where legal challenges were used to contest political changes.
distinguishing_observations: Manele's legal team has already filed preliminary motions questioning the vote's procedure.
jeremiah_manele.action_if_state: Manele will file a formal legal challenge, seek an injunction against the new Prime Minister vote, and rally international support for a review.

opposition_coalition_mps.claim: Matthew Wale is the only leader who can hold the coalition together and secure international legitimacy, so we must ensure his election.
beliefs: Wale's long opposition leadership makes him the most credible candidate
A Wale premiership will reassure aid donors and avoid diplomatic isolation
goals: Elect Wale as PM
Maintain coalition unity
Secure continued foreign aid
opposition_coalition_mps.pressures: Need to present a united front to avoid snap election and loss of power
stances: Strongly pro-Wale
Anti-Manele
Pro-diplomatic balance
historical_case_refs: Solomon Islands 2019 coalition realignment after no-confidence
distinguishing_observations: Wale is the only candidate with cross-party backing from all six coalition parties
opposition_coalition_mps.action_if_state: Lobby undecided MPs, coordinate with party whips to ensure Wale gets majority in the upcoming vote

opposition_coalition_mps.claim: Electing Wale could provoke Chinese economic retaliation, so we must instead back a candidate who will maintain close ties with Beijing.
beliefs: China will cut aid if Wale becomes PM due to his past criticism
Economic dependence on Chinese infrastructure loans is critical
goals: Avoid Chinese sanctions
Ensure continued Chinese investment
Select a PM acceptable to Beijing
opposition_coalition_mps.pressures: Threat of losing Chinese-funded projects and budget support
stances: Pro-China alignment
Skeptical of Wale
Pragmatic about aid
historical_case_refs: Solomon Islands 2019 switch from Taiwan to China
Pacific islands' aid dependency on China
distinguishing_observations: Some coalition MPs have direct ties to Chinese business interests
opposition_coalition_mps.action_if_state: Privately negotiate with pro-China factions to field an alternative candidate, possibly Frederick Kologeto, and delay the vote to build support

opposition_coalition_mps.claim: Several coalition MPs see the no-confidence vote as an opportunity to advance their own careers, so they will back whichever candidate offers them the best ministerial posts.
beliefs: Coalition loyalty is secondary to personal gain
The premiership is a bargaining chip for cabinet positions
goals: Secure lucrative ministerial portfolios
Maximize individual influence
Keep options open
opposition_coalition_mps.pressures: Short-term political survival and personal enrichment
stances: Opportunistic
Non-ideological
Factional
historical_case_refs: Solomon Islands frequent floor-crossing and coalition collapses (e.g., 2014-2019)
distinguishing_observations: Multiple MPs have switched parties in the past for personal benefit
opposition_coalition_mps.action_if_state: Engage in secret negotiations with both Wale and alternative candidates, demanding specific cabinet positions in exchange for votes, potentially splitting the coalition
